summary: Research reference models and the UK landscape across ~18 domains to scope a UK Gap Map
logs: Critic proposed 6 missing domains; researching top 4: Health and social care (including mental health), Justice, courts and access to justice, Local-state capacity, devolution and public finance, National resilience and civil preparedness (biosecurity, pandemics, climate adaptation, CNI)

essentialTechnologyPost.summary: Convergent Research's Gap Map (gap-map.org, v1.0, launched 2025, introduced in the "Essential Technology" blog post) is an interactive database of the R&D bottlenecks holding back science across roughly 20 fundamental fields. Its thesis: humanity could raise its rate of scientific and technological progress by driving resources into ~100 especially catalytic "bridge-scale" projects — mid-scale efforts costing ~$20-50M each that could unlock trillions in downstream value — which existing institutions "can't or won't build." These fall into a category the authors, borrowing Bell Labs language, call "fundamental development": the infrastructure, tools, techniques and technologies that enable entire fields but fit neither the "R" nor the "D" of R&D and are therefore systematically under-funded. Two kinds of constraint create the gaps: physical/natural limits (e.g. light scattering in brain tissue) and institutional/economic ones (how funding is organised). Maps matter, the authors argue, because they make invisible bottlenecks visible and actionable, act as a discovery/matchmaking mechanism connecting motivated researchers, funders and org-builders to unmet needs, and give researchers "permission" for out-of-the-box, deconstructive thinking. The content was built by talking to hundreds of scientists and technologists over several years — surfacing ideas from "brilliant scientists frustrated by systemic barriers" — then formalised into a database by a small team (Christina Agapakis, Murray Cox, Matt Tranzillo) with 30+ acknowledged contributors, and was explicitly modelled on Frontier Climate's Carbon Removal Knowledge Gaps database. The data model is three-tiered: R&D Gaps (bottlenecks) → Foundational Capabilities (the mid-scale infrastructure that would close a gap, ~a few hundred of them, a few dozen deemed most pivotal) → Resources (organisations/FROs/companies/nonprofits, funding programs, papers, roadmaps, whitepapers, technology seeds, and relevant researchers actively working toward each capability). Entries carry risk tags (Safety, Security, Governance/Ethics) to flag dual-use concerns, and capabilities are clustered thematically by the broad gap they address (e.g. "three-dimensional read/write access to intact biological systems" spanning neuroscience, cell biology and molecular engineering). Navigation is by "Filter by Field," category browsing, and an API & Data endpoint; a Contribute form invites new gaps, solutions, efforts and meta-analyses. The authors stress it is deliberately "a crude map of an emerging space — not a prioritization," not comprehensive, and provocative — v1.0 inviting expert correction, with future posts promised on prioritisation, differential technology development and defensive acceleration.
structure: Three-tiered linked data model, not a flat list: (1) R&D Gaps = bottlenecks/problems; (2) Foundational Capabilities = the mid-scale infrastructure/tools that would close a gap; (3) Resources = the concrete real-world efforts attached to each capability. Every entry links up/down this chain, so the map reads as gap -> what would fix it -> who is already doing something about it.

Scope quantified and bounded: ~20 fundamental fields of science; ~100 headline R&D gaps; a few hundred foundational capabilities (a few dozen flagged as most pivotal); ~10 'bridge-scale' projects. Each field carries ~3-5 pivotal pieces of infrastructure. The bounded, opinionated size is a feature — it signals curation, not an exhaustive index.

Fields are the primary organising axis and the main filter ('Filter by Field', with All/None toggles). Capabilities are additionally clustered thematically by the broad gap they serve, allowing cross-field meta-themes (e.g. 'three-dimensional read/write access to intact biological systems' spanning neuroscience, cell biology, molecular engineering).

Resource entries are typed: active entities (FROs, companies, nonprofits, research institutions), funding programs/competitions, scientific literature and reviews, roadmaps and whitepapers, technology prototypes/'seeds', and named researchers/initiators. This turns each gap into an actionable dossier rather than a description.
Cross-cutting risk tags on entries: Safety Risk, Security Risk, Governance/Ethics Risk — used to flag dual-use or grey-area items while the map otherwise focuses on 'robustly beneficial' advances.

Framing of causes: each gap is understood through two constraint types — physical/natural limits vs institutional/economic (funding/organisational) neglect — which doubles as an explanation of why the market/state hasn't already closed it.
Explicitly NOT ranked or prioritised: described as 'a crude map... not a prioritization.' Ordering is by field/theme, not by importance score; prioritisation is deferred to future work.

Openness and machine-readability: dedicated navigation for R&D Gaps, Capabilities, Resources, About, Contribute, and an 'API & Data' endpoint, so the dataset is queryable and reusable, not just a webpage.

Community contribution loop: a Contribute form and email invite submissions of new gaps, solutions and efforts, plus suggestions for better presentation and new meta-analyses — the map is positioned as a living v1.0 that improves via expert correction.
lessons: Adopt the three-tier linked model, translated to civic/economic/tech: Gap (the problem/bottleneck) -> Intervention/Capability (the mid-scale thing that would close it: an institution, dataset, standard, piece of infrastructure, policy instrument, market) -> Resources (who is already working on it — orgs, funders, pilots, reports, named people). This is the single most transferable design choice; it makes each gap actionable rather than just descriptive.

Define your own load-bearing category concept the way they coined 'fundamental development.' For the UK, name the class of thing that falls between markets and the state and is chronically under-provisioned (e.g. 'civic infrastructure', 'enabling capability', 'coordination gap'). A crisp, defensible category is what makes a map an argument rather than a directory.

Pick a clear primary organising axis and stick to it. They use ~20 scientific fields. A UK map spanning civic, economic and technological gaps needs an equivalent — e.g. domains (housing, health/social care, energy, skills, justice, local government, digital/data infrastructure) — with 'civic / economic / technological' as an orthogonal tag rather than the top-level split, so genuinely cross-cutting gaps (most real ones) aren't forced into one silo.

Bound the scope and curate hard. ~100 gaps signals judgement and keeps the map legible. Resist the urge to be exhaustive; a curated, opinionated shortlist is more useful and more credible than a giant index, and lets you explicitly flag the 'most pivotal' few dozen.

Explicitly decide whether you are mapping or prioritising, and say so. They deliberately shipped 'a map, not a prioritization' as v1.0 and deferred ranking. This lowers the bar to launch, invites correction, and avoids premature fights over what matters most — but plan the prioritisation layer (scoring, cost, tractability) as a named future phase.

Model the *cause* of each gap, not just the gap. Their physical-vs-institutional framing is powerful; a UK version should tag why each gap persists (market failure, misaligned incentives, fragmented responsibility across departments/tiers of government, missing data, regulatory barrier, funding-timescale mismatch). This turns the map into a diagnosis and points to the right kind of fix.

Attach a cross-cutting risk/consideration tagging system. They use Safety/Security/Governance-Ethics. A civic-economic map should tag e.g. equity/distributional impact, privacy/surveillance, democratic-legitimacy, and regional/'levelling-up' dimensions — so users can filter for and reason about second-order effects, and so the map isn't naively techno-solutionist.

Type your resources so each gap becomes a dossier. Mirror their resource types with UK-relevant ones: active organisations (charities, think tanks, ALBs, councils, startups), funding sources (UKRI, foundations, devolved-nation funds, local growth funds), evidence (reports, What Works Centre reviews, NAO/Select Committee findings), live pilots, and named people/champions. The value is matchmaking supply of effort to demand.

Build the contribution loop and treat v1.0 as a conversation-starter. Their map improves through expert submissions and openly asks for correction. Ship an accessible submission form, credit contributors visibly (they name 30+), and frame launch as 'help us fix this' — domain experts correcting a public map is a cheap, high-signal way to improve coverage and build a coalition.

Make it open and machine-readable from day one — provide an API/downloadable dataset, not just a website. This lets researchers, journalists and other builders run meta-analyses and cross-reference your gaps against spending, geography or outcomes, multiplying the map's reach.

Steal a proven schema rather than inventing from scratch — they explicitly copied Frontier Climate's Carbon Removal Knowledge Gaps database structure. For a UK map, look at existing evidence-gap-map conventions (e.g. 3ie/Campbell Collaboration EGMs, which use a domain x outcome matrix with colour-coded evidence density) and What Works Centres, and adapt their visual grammar so users already understand how to read it.

Ground the map in primary field research, not desk aggregation. Their entries came from years of interviews with frustrated practitioners who could name the bottleneck and the missing capability. A UK map's credibility will likewise depend on structured conversations with front-line civic, economic and technical actors, surfaced and attributed, rather than scraping existing lists.

Lead with an explicit value narrative that justifies why the map exists. They anchor everything to a headline claim ('~100 catalytic projects could raise the rate of progress', '$20-50M unlocking trillions'). A UK map needs an equivalent thesis (e.g. a bounded set of civic/economic capabilities that, if built, would disproportionately unlock growth, resilience or public value) so the map reads as a call to action with a fundable unit of work, not a catalogue of complaints.

gapMapDataModel.summary: Two resources from Convergent Research. (1) The Gap Map (gap-map.org) is an Astro site whose data is a Notion database (entity IDs are Notion page UUIDs); "gaps" are internally called "bottlenecks." It exposes three content types via a top nav — R&D Gaps, Capabilities, Resources — plus Fields as the taxonomy. Current scale: 103 gaps, 369 capabilities, 1,080 resources, 20 fields. The dashboard embeds ALL data client-side (a 2.1MB JSON blob), enabling instant search/sort/filter. A Gap has one Field, many Capabilities (avg 3.8, up to 13), free-text tags, and an editorial urgency rank. A Capability belongs back to 1–2 Gaps and links to many Resources (avg 3.1). A Resource is essentially a titled external URL with a controlled resourceType. Fields are 20 scientific disciplines, each with a description and a color gradient for visual coding. Controls: multi-select Field filter (All/None), Fuse.js fuzzy search (weighted across name/field/tags/capability names), a grid/list view toggle, a tag filter, a "private tag" banner for unpublished content, and a Sort control (rank | name | number). "Sort by rank" orders gaps by bottleneck_rank (an "Urgency: X/5" editorial score, ties broken by bottleneck_number); capabilities/resources sort by type|alpha instead. URLs: /gaps/{slug}, /capabilities/{slug}, /fields/{slug}, with ?sort=rank&... query params. (2) The /ecosystem page (convergentresearch.org) is a separate Next.js + Sanity CMS app — NOT part of the Gap Map data model. It catalogs FROs (Focused Research Organizations) as a Sanity document type `fro`, categorized by a `topic` document type. Six topic filter tabs plus "All": Mathematics, Astrophysics, Neuroscience, Climate, Biology, Artificial Intelligence. 12 FROs listed, each shown as a card with name, slug, a one-line tagline, and a single topic. FRO detail pages carry richer fields: tagline, founded year, location, website, about/mission, core technology platform, "capability targets" (focus areas), leadership (people + roles + advisory board), named funders/supporters, job listings, resources (publications/blog posts), and social links. Key insight for a UK equivalent: the Gap Map's power is a clean 4-entity relational model (Field → Gap → Capability → Resource) with one editorial urgency ranking, shipped as static embedded JSON. Organizations are deliberately kept in a SEPARATE system (the ecosystem/FRO directory) rather than joined into the gap model — orgs appear inside the gap map only indirectly, as Resources tagged type "Company"/"Research Org"/"FRO"/"Initiative"/"Funding Program".
structure: ENTITY: Gap (Notion type 'bottleneck'; 103 records; page /gaps/{slug}). Fields: id (UUID); bottleneck_name (title); bottleneck_description (long rich text, multi-paragraph); slug; bottleneck_rank (int 0-5, editorial 'Urgency /5'); bottleneck_number (stable int ID 1-104); field (single relation to Field); foundational_capabilities (array of Capability, avg 3.8, range 1-13); tags (array of strings); privateTags (array); last_edited_time (ISO8601).

ENTITY: Capability (Notion type 'foundational capability' / 'fc'; 369 records; page /capabilities/{slug}). Fields: id (UUID); fc_name (title); fc_description (rich text); slug; rank (int); resources (array of Resource, avg 3.1, range 0-22); bottlenecks (back-reference array to parent Gaps, avg 1.1, range 0-2); tags; privateTags; last_edited_time.

ENTITY: Resource (1,080 records; external references). Fields: id (UUID); resource_title; resource_url (external link, often to papers/companies); resourceTypes (array from controlled vocabulary); last_edited_time. Resources attach to Capabilities, not directly to Gaps.

ENTITY: Field / discipline taxonomy (20 records; page /fields/{slug}). Fields: id (UUID); field_name; field_description (rich text intro to the discipline); colorName + colorClass (e.g. 'field-gradient-14', for consistent color coding across cards); last_edited_time. Each Gap belongs to exactly one Field.

CONTROLLED VOCAB resourceTypes (11 values, with counts): Research and Reviews (336), Initiative (195), Whitepapers and Essays (141), Company (134), Technology Seed (104), Funding Program (59), Research Org (58), Roadmap (24), Individual (11), FRO (10), FRO Concept (8). NOTE: organizations/companies/funders live here as Resources, not as a first-class entity.

CONTROLLED VOCAB tags (cross-cutting, applied to Gaps & Capabilities): Security Risk, Safety Risk, Governance/Ethics Risk, Longevity and Aging, Brain-Body Interactions, Origin of Life, Recycling. (Separate from Fields; used for filtering + fuzzy search.)

THE 20 FIELDS: Neuroscience, Cellular and Molecular Biology, Physiology and Medicine, Immunology, Metascience, Mechanical Engineering, Synthetic Biology, Astrophysics, Computation, Ecology, Physics, Social Science, Chemistry, Biosecurity, Biophysics, Geophysics and Climate, Global Health, Nanoscale Fabrication, Space Engineering, Materials Science.

RELATIONSHIPS: Field 1—* Gap (each Gap has one Field, color-inherited). Gap 1—* Capability (foundational_capabilities; a Gap's card shows 'Show N related Capabilities', expandable inline). Capability *—1..2 Gap (back-link array). Capability 1—* Resource. So the join graph is Field → Gap → Capability → Resource, a strict 4-level hierarchy with no direct Gap↔Resource or Gap↔Org edge.

FILTERS / CONTROLS (Gap Map): (a) 'Filter by Field' multi-select checklist of 20 fields with All/None buttons; (b) free-text Search using Fuse.js fuzzy matching, field weights: name .5, field_name .3, tags .4, foundational_capabilities.fc_name .4 + fc_description, bottleneck_rank .2, bottleneck_number .2; (c) grid/list View toggle; (d) tag filter (selectedTag) and a Private-Tag banner exposing unpublished/preview content via ?privateTag; (e) Sort control.

SORT-BY-RANK MECHANICS: URL ?sort=rank is the default. For Gaps, 'rank' sorts by bottleneck_rank descending (the 0-5 'Urgency' score; capability cards render 'Urgency: X/5'), ties broken by bottleneck_number ascending. Other sort keys: 'name' (localeCompare alphabetical) and 'number' (bottleneck_number). Per-view options: Gaps = [rank, alpha]; Capabilities = [type, alpha]; Resources = [type, alpha]. Ranking is editorial/manual, curated in the CMS, not computed.

DELIVERY ARCHITECTURE (Gap Map): Astro SSR + hydrated islands (FieldFilter, Search, SortControl, ViewToggle, DashboardGrid). The ENTIRE dataset (all gaps/capabilities/resources/fields with nested relations) is serialized into the DashboardGrid component props as one ~2MB JSON payload, so all filtering/sorting/search happens client-side with no API. Source of truth = Notion.

SEPARATE ENTITY: FRO / Organisation (convergentresearch.org/ecosystem — Next.js + Sanity CMS, distinct app). Sanity type 'fro'; page /ecosystem/{slug}. Listing-card fields: name, slug, one-line tagline/description, single topic (category). Detail-page fields: name, tagline, founded year, location, website URL, about/mission (rich text), core technology/platform name, 'capability targets' (array of focus areas), leadership (people with roles: founders, CEO/COO, Scientific Advisory Board), named funders/supporters (array), job listings, resources (publications + blog posts), social links.

ORG CATEGORY taxonomy (Sanity type 'topic'): fields _id, name, slug.current, _createdAt/_updatedAt. Six topics used as filter tabs (plus 'All'): Mathematics, Astrophysics, Neuroscience, Climate, Biology, Artificial Intelligence. Each FRO is tagged with one topic; the page groups/filters FROs by topic tab + a field-filter dropdown. 12 FROs currently: CHI-FRO, Cultivarium, [C]Worthy, Dragonfly, E11 Bio, Echo Labs, EvE Bio, Forest Neurotech, Imprint, Lean FRO, Meridial, Parallel Squared Technology Institute.

CROSS-RESOURCE INSIGHT: the Gap Map and the Ecosystem/FRO directory are TWO separate systems on different stacks (Astro+Notion vs Next+Sanity) with DIFFERENT category taxonomies (20 scientific Fields vs 6 broad Topics). Organizations are intentionally NOT modeled as nodes in the gap graph; they surface in the gap map only as typed Resources ('Company','Research Org','FRO','Initiative','Funding Program').
lessons: Adopt the 4-entity hierarchy as your spine: Field (discipline) → Gap → Capability → Resource. It is clean, it renders as expandable cards (Gap card shows count of child Capabilities), and it cleanly separates the problem (Gap) from the proposed solution (Capability) from the evidence (Resource). A UK gap map should reuse exactly this shape.

Give every Gap a single Field (for color-coded, mutually-exclusive categorization and filtering) PLUS a multi-value free-text tag list (for cross-cutting themes like 'Security Risk', 'Net Zero', 'Sovereign Capability'). One-of taxonomy for color/filter + many-of tags for nuance is the pattern that works.

Rank is editorial, not algorithmic: store an integer urgency/priority score (0-5) on each Gap, curated in the CMS, and make 'sort by rank' the default view. Break ties with a stable sequential number field. Do not try to compute rank — a hand-curated priority order is the product's core opinion. Display it prominently (e.g. 'Urgency: 4/5').

Keep two distinct stable identifiers per Gap: a semantic slug for URLs (/gaps/{slug}) and an immutable integer 'number' as the human-facing ID and tie-breaker. Ranks can be re-ordered without changing the number.

Model Resources as lightweight typed links, not heavyweight records: just title + external URL + a controlled 'resourceType'. A rich resourceType vocabulary (paper, review, company, initiative, funding program, roadmap, whitepaper, org, individual) lets one resource table serve papers, companies AND funders — avoiding premature separate tables.

Attach Resources to Capabilities, not to Gaps, and let a Capability back-link to its parent Gap(s). This keeps evidence next to the specific proposed solution and keeps Gap descriptions clean/narrative.

Do NOT fold organisations into the gap data model. Convergent keeps its FRO/org directory in a completely separate app. In the gap map, orgs appear only as typed Resources. For a UK version, a separate 'UK org/ecosystem directory' with its own richer schema (funding, leadership, location, founded year) is the right architecture; link the two loosely via resourceType='Company'/'Org'.

For an org/ecosystem directory, the useful per-org fields to collect are: name, slug, one-line tagline, single category/topic, website, founded year, location, mission (rich text), focus areas / capability targets (list), leadership (people + roles + advisory board), funders/supporters (list), open roles, and publications — mirror the FRO detail page.

Use a small broad topic taxonomy (~6 tabs) for the org directory but a larger fine-grained field taxonomy (~20 disciplines) for the gaps. Different granularities serve different audiences; don't force one shared taxonomy across both.

Ship the whole dataset as embedded static JSON and do all search/sort/filter client-side. At this scale (~100 gaps, ~370 capabilities, ~1000 resources = ~2MB) there is no need for a backend API; a static build from a CMS (Notion/Sanity/Airtable) gives instant UX and trivial hosting.

Implement search as weighted fuzzy matching (Fuse.js-style) across name, description, field, tags, and child capability names/descriptions — so a query hits a Gap via its solutions too, not just its title.

Build a 'private tag' / preview mechanism into the schema from day one (a privateTags field + a banner) so draft or embargoed gaps can be shared with reviewers via a URL param before public launch — useful for a governance/consultation workflow.
Persist all view state in the URL query string (?sort=rank, field filters, tag, search) so specific filtered views are shareable and linkable — important for policy/stakeholder communication.

Give each Field a color gradient and a descriptive intro paragraph with its own page (/fields/{slug}). The color coding threads visual identity through every card and the field pages double as topic landing pages for SEO and orientation.

europe2031.summary:
"Europe 2031 — What getting AI wrong means for us" (europe2031.ai; PDF snapshot 29 June 2026; 68pp) is scenario fiction by eight European AI-policy figures: Daan Juijn (research director, Arq Foundation, Brussels, which funded the site/translations), Stan van Baarsen (Delta Institute; Dutch National AI Plan), Judith Dada (VC general partner; advises German government), Maximilian Negele and Lily Stelling (Oxford Martin AI Governance Initiative), Philip Fox (KIRA Center; International AI Safety Report), Alex Petropoulos (Arq chief of staff), Michiel Bakker (MIT professor; Google DeepMind), edited by Tom Chivers. They claim no AI-industry funding, personal capacities.

Told through fictional Brussels official Caroline Dubois and expat founder Christian Vogt, it runs Jan 2025–Jun 2026 on real events (DeepSeek R1, the largely illusory EUR 200bn InvestAI, Gigafactory delays, a US executive order screening frontier models pre-release), then speculates to 2031 with fictional labs Atlas (US), Helios (EU), Zimo (China). The chain: US compute advantage (17→255 GW vs Europe 1.4→21 GW) compounds via AI-automated R&D; open-weight cyber models unleash ransomware that hits "Buy European" agencies hardest; Washington rations frontier inference (FISR tiers — Five Eyes, Japan, Korea, Netherlands Tier 1; most of the EU Tier 2); the EU's Anti-Coercion Instrument fails a qualified-majority vote; continually-learning agents gut white-collar and graduate hiring; tax bases erode as value flows to US firms via low-tax jurisdictions; France is downgraded, the euro cracks; China buys influence via loans to southern Europe; the US coerces the sale of a distressed German automaker to Atlas; endgame — Washington demands control of ASML, Europe's only chokepoint, while Beijing counter-threatens rare earths and robot licences.

The epilogue (2034 retrospective) carries the recommendations: tens-of-gigawatt Special Compute Zones with three-month permitting, partnering US hyperscalers but anchoring assets under European law; a middle-power coalition (NL, DE, FR, Norway, UK, Canada, Japan, S. Korea) pooling supply-chain leverage; robotics/world-model industrialisation plus investment screening; Danish-style flexicurity; a positive public vision; leverage as the only route to slowing the race.

The UK fares comparatively well: its AI Security Institute is the sole European body in the cyber-defence coalition, the UK is Tier 1 via Five Eyes, and post-Brexit it brokers bilateral US deals — but that status is discretionary, and the economic, fiscal and labour mechanisms fully apply to services-heavy Britain.

Credibility: well-credentialed authors, mechanisms grounded in real precedent (FDPR/Huawei), honest about speculating — but single-scenario advocacy assuming fast timelines, lab-sympathetic framing, and a deregulatory policy lean.
structure: Chronological dated chapters (Jan 2025 - Mar 2031), each headed by month + title, with a recurring quantitative sidebar tracking US vs Europe installed AI compute in GW and the US 'compute advantage' multiple (12.4x -> 9x -> 12.3x) as the single through-line metric.

Three-part arc with an explicit epistemic status marker: prologue of real, verifiable events using real companies (Jan 2025 - Jun 2026); a flagged 'from here we speculate' scenario (Aug 2026 - Mar 2031) using anonymised composite actors — Atlas (leading US lab), Helios (leading EU lab), Zimo (leading Chinese lab); and a 2034 epilogue.

Two viewpoint characters bridge worlds — a Brussels DG TRADE official and a German founder in San Francisco — with recurring text-message exchanges used as fast commentary/foreshadowing between narrative sections.

Entity types woven through: states (US, China, EU27, UK, Japan, Korea), labs, chokepoint firms (ASML, TSMC, NVIDIA), and policy instruments (FISR inference-tiering, US pre-release screening EO, EU Digital Sovereignty Regulation, Anti-Coercion Instrument, Project Glasswing, Frontier AI Initiative).

Recommendations are not a standalone list; they are embedded as counterfactuals in the epilogue's retrospective AI-conducted interview (Special Compute Zones, middle-power coalition, robotics/world-models, flexicurity, positive vision, contingency plans).

Published as a live multi-language website (with a markdown version advertised 'for AI agents') plus a generated PDF snapshot; no footnotes or citations — pre-2026 claims are traceable to news events but not referenced.

Causal ranking implicit in the narrative: compute -> model access -> cyber/economic dependence -> fiscal erosion -> political fragmentation -> loss of the last chokepoint (ASML); each chapter escalates one link of the chain.
lessons: Make compute the master variable: encode UK-jurisdiction AI compute (GW, installed vs announced vs delivered) against US/China trend lines; the report's sidebar shows Europe growing 1.4->21 GW while the gap still widens — a UK map should track relative share and delivery slippage, not announcements.

Distinguish location from control: datacentres on UK soil owned by US hyperscalers remain commandeerable via US law (the EO and FISR mechanisms); the gap is capacity 'bolted to the floor' — anchored under UK law, contracts, and energy supply.

Model frontier-model access as a revocable licence: the UK's Tier 1/Five Eyes treatment in the scenario is a discretionary Washington call 'every three months, forever'; encode the absence of guaranteed-access mechanisms (treaty-level or long-term contractual) and a trigger scenario for inference rationing or price shocks.

Map cyber-defence access below the elite tier: the ransomware wave hits institutions without frontier-class defence — hospitals, councils, universities; AISI access is not NHS access; encode which UK public institutions could obtain frontier cyberdefence when offensive capability proliferates via open weights.

Weight white-collar services exposure heavily: displacement lands first on law, consulting, finance, software and graduate intake — the UK's economic core; encode sector/region exposure with entry-level hiring as the leading indicator (the report's 'worst graduate market in living memory').

Encode the fiscal transmission gap: AI value captured by US firms routed through low-tax jurisdictions erodes the tax base exactly as welfare demand and debt-service costs rise; map UK dependence on high-earner PAYE and services corporation tax, and gilt-market headroom.

Include labour-transition machinery as a named gap: the report's fix is Danish flexicurity — wage insurance, retraining and displaced-worker support paired with firing flexibility to enable deep adoption; the UK has neither the insurance layer nor an adoption strategy tied to it.

Build a leverage inventory: the UK holds no ASML-class chokepoint, so it has less coercive protection than the EU; its actual levers are talent, AISI evaluation capacity, finance, and diplomacy — map each lever and the missing strategy to use them, including the report's explicitly UK-inclusive middle-power coalition (NL, DE, FR, Norway, Canada, Japan, Korea).

Cover asset retention: distressed high-tech firms and successful public-programme researchers are acquired the moment they matter (Atlas buying the automaker and poaching Frontier Initiative staff); map NSI Act screening coverage against AI-era assets (robotics, world models, chip IP) and compute-budget-based talent raids.

Encode institutional cognition as a first-order gap: the report's root failure is governments unable to perceive or act at AI speed — civil servants banned from frontier tools, evidence standards demanding peer review during monthly capability jumps; measure government frontier-tool access, evaluation capacity, and decision latency versus model release cadence.

Require contingency plans ('Plan B'): the scenario's sovereignty policies assumed success and had no fallback when the domestic champion lagged; encode trigger-based contingencies for access cut-off, key-firm acquisition, and ally rationing — the memo Caroline wrote and Brussels shelved.

Track legitimacy and benefit-sharing: anti-AI backlash with no positive vision destroys political room in every country in the scenario, while the US buys time with AI-funded cash transfers; map UK mechanisms for distributing AI gains and public-attitude data as a gap in its own right.

Include energy and permitting concretely: the benchmark is permitting cut from two years to three months, concierge teams, converted decommissioned plants and new generation; encode UK grid-connection queues and planning timelines for AI Growth Zones against that bar (note the scenario has OpenAI abandoning a UK datacentre over 'regulatory hurdles' and Fluidstack leaving London).

Add a physical-AI dependency axis: China's humanoid-robotics scale becomes a second coercion channel (rare earths, robot export licences); map UK robotics manufacturing capacity and supply-chain exposure separately from cognitive-AI dependence.

Measure gaps in capability-time, not static rank: Helios stays a constant '18 months behind' while the absolute gap explodes because release cycles compress; a UK gap map should encode refresh cadence and time-to-frontier, and re-score frequently rather than ranking once.

crMethodology.summary:
Convergent Research (CR), the nonprofit founded 2021 by Adam Marblestone and Anastasia Gamick that designs/launches Focused Research Organizations (FROs), published the "Fundamental Development Gap Map" v1.0 on 15 April 2025 at gap-map.org. Lead authors: Adam Marblestone, Mary Wang, Joseph Fridman, with 30+ external contributors. It maps "fundamental development" gaps — mid-scale ("bridge-scale") infrastructure, tools, datasets and techniques that whole fields need but individual labs, academia or VC won't build (analogues to telescopes, accelerators, the Human Genome Project).

Scale/thesis: ~20 scientific fields, each with ~3-5 pivotal gaps, implying ~100 bridge-scale projects at ~$20-50M each. The live database holds roughly 101 R&D gaps, ~363 foundational capabilities and ~977 resources.

Sourcing: gaps come from "hundreds of conversations" / interviews with scientists and technologists over several years ("catalytic ideas... from brilliant scientists frustrated by systemic barriers"), plus a "detector-and-predictor" model — open calls/solicitations (~100 applications reviewed), sponsored workshops, and roadmapping fellows. Community input via a Contribute form, gapmap@convergentresearch.org, and social channels. Two structural lenses guide gap-finding: physical/first-principles constraints and the political economy of research institutions.

Prioritisation: deliberately NOT ranked in v1.0 — "a crude map of an emerging space, not a prioritization of where you should go inside it." No scores. They flag future posts on prioritisation frameworks (differential technology development, defensive acceleration). Entries carry three risk tags — Safety, Security, Governance/Ethics — and they "mostly focus on robustly beneficial advancements."

Governance/cadence: thinly specified. It is explicitly community-iterative and editorially owned by CR, but there is no published curator list, review workflow, per-entry provenance, or committed update schedule — a notable weakness. Versioned as v1.0.

Stated limitations (prominent and repeated): "not by any means a comprehensive survey or prioritized roadmap"; "isn't comprehensive - at all"; inevitably incomplete; illustrative of a category rather than exhaustive.

FRO link: gaps are meant to seed coordinated programs, but CR explicitly de-couples the map from FROs — solutions may be "FRO-shaped and otherwise," including ARPA programs, companies, prize competitions and academia. FROs themselves: nonprofit startups, 10-30 FTE, $20-50M, 3-7 years, ~a dozen launched since 2021.

Sources: gap-map.org/about and /resources; essentialtechnology.blog "Introducing the Convergent Research Gap Map"; issues.org FRO field notes (Marblestone/Gamick/Wang/Fridman); MIT News 2025-06-06.
structure: Three-tier linked data model: (1) R&D Gaps (~101) — broad 'fundamental development' bottlenecks; (2) Foundational Capabilities (~363) — the mid-scale tools/tech/data/infrastructure that would close a gap; (3) Resources (~977) — the concrete evidence and actors tied to each capability. Capabilities are 'thematically organized by which broad R&D gap they address', and resources hang off capabilities.

Top-level partition by scientific field: ~20 fields, each expected to hold ~3-5 pivotal gaps. Users browse by field, by gap, or by keyword, or explore a graph/network view showing the gap-capability-resource links.

Resource entries are heterogeneous and typed: active entities (FROs, companies, nonprofits, research institutions), funding programs/competitions, scientific literature (reviews, papers), roadmaps, whitepapers/speculative pieces, technology prototypes/proofs-of-concept, and individual researchers/project initiators.
Risk taxonomy applied as tags across entries: Safety Risk, Security Risk, Governance/Ethics Risk — used to caveat rather than to rank.

No ranking or scoring layer by design: v1.0 is explicitly descriptive ('crude map... not a prioritization'). No numeric priority, urgency or impact score is exposed, though they signal intent to add 'attributes to help orient users to urgency or potential impact' later.

Contribution/feedback layer bolted onto the browse model: a Contribute submission form plus email and social channels feed candidate gaps/capabilities/resources, editorially owned by Convergent Research.

Framing/entry documents: a conceptual blog post ('fundamental development', 'bridge-scale', $20-50M sizing), an About page (contents + how to use), and a Resources page — these carry the methodology and limitations narrative rather than per-entry metadata.
Versioning: released and labelled as v1.0, positioning the whole resource as an evolving artifact rather than a fixed reference.
lessons: Name an accountable editorial owner and publish the curation workflow up front. The CR map's biggest governance weakness is that it under-specifies who curates, how submissions are vetted, and how conflicts are handled. A UK map should designate a curator/editorial board, publish acceptance criteria, and log contributor identity so the map reads as a neutral reference, not a pipeline for one funder.

Separate description from prioritisation, and stage them deliberately. CR shipped a descriptive 'crude map, not a prioritization' first and reserved ranking for later. Copy this: launch descriptive, and when you add ranking make the criteria explicit, dated, and visibly subjective rather than letting a score masquerade as objective truth.

Triangulate gap-sourcing to avoid capture and advocacy. CR combined interviews with two structural lenses — first-principles physical constraints and the political economy of institutions — plus open calls (~100 applications) and workshops. Relying only on enthusiastic expert interviews biases toward well-connected fields; a UK map should cross-check nominations against independent evidence and gap-analysis, and cap self-nomination.

Version and date everything, with per-entry provenance and a committed refresh cadence. CR labelled the whole map v1.0 but did NOT publish a per-entry 'last reviewed' date or an update schedule — the main staleness risk. A UK map should stamp each entry with source, contributor, date added, and last-verified, and commit publicly to a review cycle (e.g. quarterly or biannual).

Build in a lifecycle/retirement process for entries tied to live initiatives. Because resources point at real organizations and programs that get funded, completed, or shut down, entries decay into 'stale advocacy' fast. Assign owners to mark initiatives completed/defunct and to demote gaps that have been substantially addressed, so the map tracks reality rather than boosting yesterday's asks.

Keep contribution frictionless but gated by editorial review. CR's Contribute-form-plus-email model lowers the barrier; pair that openness with a clear review gate and published inclusion standards so the map grows without becoming a noticeboard or a lobbying surface.

De-couple gaps from any single funding vehicle. CR explicitly says solutions can be 'FRO-shaped and otherwise' — ARPA-style programs, companies, prizes, academia. A UK map should describe gaps mechanism-agnostically; tying every gap to one instrument (or one organisation's programme) is what turns a map into advocacy.

Use a linked graph data model (gap to capability to resource to field) rather than flat lists. It keeps entries navigable, forces de-duplication, and makes coverage gaps visible — but budget for the maintenance overhead that graph curation demands.

Tag risks and caveats on entries (safety, security, governance/ethics). CR's three-tag system signals critical distance and counters uncritical techno-optimism; a UK map, especially if public-facing, should carry comparable caveats plus explicit limitations text ('not comprehensive, not a ranked roadmap') to set expectations and invite correction.

State limitations loudly and repeatedly. CR foregrounds incompleteness ('not by any means a comprehensive survey', 'isn't comprehensive - at all'). Prominent, honest scoping language protects credibility, deflects the 'why isn't X here' critique, and actively recruits corrections — a cheap but powerful anti-staleness mechanism.

domains.domain: Civic action and revitalisation of civil society
domains.landscape: UK associational life continues its long thinning: monthly formal volunteering fell from 27% (2013/14) to 17% (2024/25, Community Life Survey); union density is 22.4%, roughly half its historic peak, though public-sector membership is rising; most denominations report ageing membership (the Bible Society's 'Quiet Revival' growth claim was withdrawn in March 2026 after its survey proved faulty). Around 7% of adults report chronic loneliness. Yet 2025–26 is an unusually active policy window: the Civil Society Covenant (July 2025) reset government–sector relations; the English Devolution and Community Empowerment Act 2026 created a Community Right to Buy and a neighbourhood governance duty; Pride in Place committed up to £5bn over ten years to ~379 deprived neighbourhoods; the £175m Community Wealth Fund launches in 2026; and a place-based philanthropy roadmap appeared in April 2026. Meanwhile load-bearing institutions have exited: the What Works Centre for Wellbeing and Campaign to End Loneliness (2024), National Citizen Service (2025), Big Local/Local Trust and the Know Your Neighbourhood Fund (2026), and the Community Ownership Fund (closed 2024 with £15m unspent). Community business is growing (869 trading, ~1,000 projects in development) and 40+ councils have run citizens' assemblies, but civic tech, local news (4.4m people live in news deserts) and community organising remain subscale and grant-fragile. The pattern: new rights and neighbourhood money are arriving faster than the capital funds, capacity institutions and evidence infrastructure needed to make them work.

actors.name: Citizens UK
actors.note: Largest broad-based community organising alliance: 19 chapters, 100+ staff, funded by member dues plus a ~£5m/9-year National Lottery Solidarity Fund grant.

actors.name: ACORN UK
actors.note: Member-funded community/tenant union organising low-income residents in 10+ cities since 2014; one of few dues-funded civic organising models.

actors.name: Community Organisers Ltd
actors.note: Runs the National Academy of Community Organising (2,000+ learners); legacy body of the 2011–15 government programme that trained 500 organisers.

actors.name: mySociety
actors.note: Core UK civic tech: TheyWorkForYou, WhatDoTheyKnow, FixMyStreet; its Shifting Landscapes report (Jan 2026) maps the fragile pro-democracy tech sector.

actors.name: Democracy Club
actors.note: Open election data (candidates, polling stations); Electoral Commission is taking the polling station finder in-house, exposing the rest of its data work.

actors.name: Involve
actors.note: UK's main public-participation/deliberation practice body; ran national citizens' assemblies and supports councils regularising deliberative engagement.

actors.name: Local Trust
actors.note: Delivered Big Local (£217m, 150 resident-led areas, 2011–26) and the Community Leadership Academy; closes in 2026, leaving a capacity vacuum.

actors.name: Locality
actors.note: Network of community organisations/anchors; key advocate on assets of community value and the Community Right to Buy implementation.

actors.name: Plunkett UK
actors.note: Supports 869 community-owned businesses (shops, pubs); documents post-COF funding barriers and calls for a rural community ownership fund.

actors.name: Power to Change
actors.note: Endowed (dormant-assets-origin) funder and evidence body for community business; co-backer of the We're Right Here community power campaign.

actors.name: Public Interest News Foundation
actors.note: Maps UK news deserts (4.4m people, 37 districts), runs the Local News Commission and Local News Fund for independent outlets.

actors.name: UK Community Foundations
actors.note: 47 accredited community foundations; delivery infrastructure for place-based giving and programmes like Know Your Neighbourhood.

actors.name: National Lottery Community Fund
actors.note: Largest community funder; delivering the £175m Community Wealth Fund from 2026 and dormant-assets community programmes.

actors.name: NCVO
actors.note: Voluntary sector umbrella; its Civil Society Almanac is the main sector dataset but lags 3–4 years and was delayed to 2026.

actors.name: Jo Cox Foundation
actors.note: Convenes post-2024 loneliness/social connection policy work after the Campaign to End Loneliness and What Works Centre for Wellbeing closed.

gaps.title: No acquisition capital behind the new Community Right to Buy
gaps.description: The English Devolution and Community Empowerment Act 2026 gives communities first refusal on assets of community value, but the Community Ownership Fund closed in December 2024 (£135m to 409 projects; £15m unspent) and government explicitly declined to replace it. Partial coverage: the Community Wealth Fund (£175m, but restricted to ~doubly-disadvantaged neighbourhoods and not asset-purchase-focused), community shares (typical raises too small for market-value purchases), Architectural Heritage Fund loans, and Plunkett/Locality advice. None provides ready capital within the statutory moratorium window, so the new right risks being unusable precisely where assets matter most.
gaps.why_it_matters: A statutory right without purchase capital is dead letter: communities must raise market value within a fixed moratorium. Plunkett reports the funding vacuum is already stopping groups saving local businesses, and deprived areas — least able to crowdfund — lose assets first, deepening the social-infrastructure divide the Act was meant to close.
gaps.what_would_fill_it: A permanent Community Asset Acquisition Fund (grants + patient loans + community-shares match, able to underwrite bids within the moratorium period), capitalised at £50–100m/year from dormant assets, Pride in Place capital allocations or a National Lottery partnership; delivered through community foundations with Plunkett/Locality wraparound support.

gaps.title: Community rights stop at assets: no right to shape services or control investment
gaps.description: The We're Right Here campaign (Young Foundation, Power to Change, People's Health Trust and community leaders) sought three rights; the 2026 Act delivered only the Community Right to Buy. There is no Community Right to Shape Public Services (co-design/challenge of local services) and no Right to Control Investment (community sign-off on neighbourhood regeneration spend). The Act's neighbourhood governance duty on councils exists but its parameters are left entirely to future regulations, and the Localism Act 2011's community right to challenge was little-used and remains unreformed.
gaps.why_it_matters: Pride in Place hands 379 neighbourhood boards up to £20m each, but residents still have no enforceable standing over the services and investment decisions that dominate local outcomes. Without completing the rights framework, neighbourhood governance risks repeating the consultative, easily-ignored structures that discredited earlier regeneration programmes.
gaps.what_would_fill_it: Regulations under the 2026 Act (or a successor Community Power Act) specifying neighbourhood governance with teeth: a community right to co-design/trigger review of local public services, a participatory budgeting duty for defined local investment streams, and statutory standing for recognised neighbourhood bodies — as blueprinted in We're Right Here's January 2026 neighbourhood governance proposal.

gaps.title: No at-scale, independent financing instrument for local public-interest news
gaps.description: PINF's December 2025 Local News Report finds 4.4m people in news deserts (37 districts: 27 with no outlet, 10 with limited coverage), concentrated in deprived and diverse areas. The BBC-funded Local Democracy Reporting Service (~165 reporters, ~£8m/year) has no guaranteed future amid £500m BBC cuts and Charter review; DCMS's Amplify plan offers only £6m in 2026/27 plus up to £6m in 2027/28. PINF's own Local News Fund is philanthropic and small; Google's estimated £2.2bn UK news-derived revenue is uncaptured. Charitable status for journalism remains legally awkward, deterring foundation funding.
gaps.why_it_matters: Local news is the accountability layer for the entire devolution agenda — new strategic authorities, mayors and neighbourhood boards will spend billions with fewer reporters watching. Evidence links news provision to local service performance and civic participation; deserts map onto exactly the deprived areas receiving Pride in Place money.
gaps.what_would_fill_it: An independently governed Local News Fund/endowment at £30–50m per year, financed by a platform levy or negotiated platform contributions plus philanthropy; LDRS protected and expanded in the BBC Charter settlement (extending reporters to courts and NHS bodies); and Law Commission/Charity Commission clarification making public-interest journalism a charitable purpose.

gaps.title: No evidence institution for social connection, loneliness and civic health
gaps.description: The What Works Centre for Wellbeing closed on 30 April 2024, taking the Campaign to End Loneliness (hosted since 2021) with it; both websites are now static archives. DCMS's loneliness team was repurposed in 2020 and ministerial focus has faded. The £34.5m Know Your Neighbourhood Fund — the largest recent experiment in volunteering/loneliness interventions in 27 deprived areas — ended in March 2026 with its evaluation published but no institutional owner to carry findings forward. The Jo Cox Foundation's policy group and academic centres partially cover, but nothing synthesises evidence, sets standards or advises funders at national scale.
gaps.why_it_matters: Government and funders are about to spend billions on neighbourhood renewal (Pride in Place, Community Wealth Fund) whose stated aims include social connection — with no institution able to say what works. Evidence from a decade of loneliness programmes is fragmenting exactly as demand for it peaks.
gaps.what_would_fill_it: A What Works Centre for Social Connection and Civic Life with 10-year endowed funding (ESRC + DCMS + foundations, mirroring other What Works Centres), formally owning the KYN evidence base, Community Life Survey analysis, and evaluation duties written into Pride in Place and Community Wealth Fund delivery.

gaps.title: No official statistics on social infrastructure and associational life
gaps.description: The UK has no official, regular, neighbourhood-level measure of social infrastructure stock or associational membership. The Community Life Survey is England-only and cannot resolve below regional level; NCVO's Almanac lags 3–4 years and its publication slipped to 2026; the Bible Society's 'Quiet Revival' withdrawal (March 2026, faulty YouGov data) showed how weak religious-participation data is. Onward's Social Fabric Index (last full update 2023) and OCSI/Local Trust's Community Needs Index are one-off or proprietary. The British Academy/Bennett Institute measurement project is foundational research, not an operational statistical product; the Community Wealth Fund had to commission bespoke analysis just to find 'doubly disadvantaged' areas.
gaps.why_it_matters: Billions in neighbourhood funding (Pride in Place, Community Wealth Fund) are now allocated using ad hoc, non-official indices that cannot be scrutinised or updated. Without baseline statistics, none of these ten-year programmes can credibly demonstrate whether Britain's civic fabric is recovering or fraying.
gaps.what_would_fill_it: ONS-badged annual social infrastructure and civic health statistics at neighbourhood (LSOA/ward) level — a maintained open register of community spaces and associations plus participation measures — co-developed with the Bennett Institute/British Academy programme, OCSI and Onward, and mandated as the evaluation baseline for government neighbourhood programmes.

gaps.title: No national capacity institution for neighbourhood governance and resident leadership
gaps.description: From April 2026, ~379 Pride in Place neighbourhood boards begin spending up to £20m each, and the 2026 Act obliges councils to establish neighbourhood governance arrangements — but the infrastructure that trained resident leaders is disappearing. Local Trust (whose Community Leadership Academy supported leaders across 150 Big Local areas and helped mobilise 6,000+ first-time volunteers) winds down in 2026; the government's community organiser training programmes ended years ago; Community Organisers Ltd's academy is small; Locality supports organisations rather than resident boards. JRF has already flagged capacity gaps as the programme's key risk. No body is charged with training, peer networks, or governance support for thousands of new resident decision-makers.
gaps.why_it_matters: Big Local's core lesson is that resident-led funding works only with long-term capacity support and conflict-resolution help. Without a successor institution, Pride in Place boards in the most deprived neighbourhoods — many with no governance experience — are the likeliest point of failure for a £5bn, ten-year programme.
gaps.what_would_fill_it: A National Neighbourhood Academy: a 10-year funded institution (MHCLG + National Lottery + foundations) providing training, accreditation, peer networks and troubleshooting for resident board members and neighbourhood committees, explicitly inheriting Local Trust's Community Leadership Academy methods and Big Local evidence base.

gaps.title: A Covenant without teeth, alongside unreformed advocacy law
gaps.description: The Civil Society Covenant (July 2025) is non-statutory, with no adjudication or redress when public bodies breach its principles; its Local Covenant Partnerships Fund (£11.59m) covers just 15 of 300+ English local authority areas. Meanwhile the legal environment contradicts the partnership rhetoric: the Lobbying Act 2014's Part 2 rules still chill charity campaigning (government has rejected amendment calls), CIVICUS has rated UK civic space 'obstructed' since 2023, and Human Rights Watch (January 2026) documents escalating protest restrictions. NCVO, ACEVO and Bond monitor and advocate, but no independent mechanism exists to enforce the government–civil society relationship or audit civic-space health.
gaps.why_it_matters: The Covenant is the government's flagship civil-society policy; if it remains exhortatory while advocacy and protest law tightens, trust between state and sector — already damaged — will erode further, and charities will keep self-censoring on policy issues where their front-line evidence matters most.
gaps.what_would_fill_it: A statutory duty on public bodies to have regard to Covenant principles, with an independent review/ombudsman function reporting to Parliament; reform of Lobbying Act Part 2 (raising thresholds, clarifying issue-based campaigning); and a funded rollout of local covenant partnerships beyond the 15 pilot areas.

gaps.title: No maintenance funding for democratic digital infrastructure (no civic Sovereign Tech Fund)
gaps.description: The UK's democratic data and civic tech layer is maintained by a handful of fragile organisations: mySociety (TheyWorkForYou, WhatDoTheyKnow, FixMyStreet) cross-subsidises via consultancy and short grants; Democracy Club crowd-sources candidate and results data that the state does not publish, and the Electoral Commission is absorbing only the polling-station finder. mySociety's Shifting Landscapes report (January 2026) and Careful Industries both conclude there is no systematic support for public-interest technology in the UK — unlike Germany's Sovereign Tech Fund, which pays for maintenance of critical open infrastructure. Grant funders prefer novelty over maintenance, so load-bearing civic services live perpetually one funding cycle from shutdown.
gaps.why_it_matters: Parliament-monitoring, FOI and election-information services are used by millions and underpin journalism, research and voter participation. Their collapse would be invisible until an election. Maintenance-blind funding also wastes public value: the state repeatedly rebuilds what civic tech already runs, at higher cost.
gaps.what_would_fill_it: A Democratic Digital Infrastructure Fund (£10–20m/year, DSIT/DCMS + foundations, modelled on Germany's Sovereign Tech Fund) paying for maintenance and security of critical civic codebases and datasets; plus statutory duties to publish open candidate, election-results and council-decision data, removing the volunteer burden Democracy Club currently carries.

gaps.title: No participation pipeline: volunteering infrastructure and a successor to NCS
gaps.description: Monthly formal volunteering has fallen from 27% (2013/14) to 17% (2024/25); youth movements ration participation for lack of adults (Scouts received 5,000+ unanswered joining enquiries in 2024; waiting lists persist). The National Citizen Service closed in March 2025 — ending the only national civic service programme and unrenewed funding for ~250 youth organisations — and the National Youth Strategy (late 2025) funds facilities (£85m+, Better Youth Spaces) but offers no universal service/volunteering pathway. The sector-led Vision for Volunteering has no delivery funding. Practical friction persists: no portable vetting/DBS passport, no recognised volunteering credential, weak employer-supported volunteering incentives. Royal Voluntary Service, NCVO and volunteer centres cover fragments only.
gaps.why_it_matters: Volunteering is the entry drug of civic life; its decade-long decline among working-age adults feeds every other gap in this map. The state just dismantled its only youth civic-service institution without replacement, while demand (waiting lists, first-time volunteers in KYN areas) shows supply, not appetite, is the binding constraint.
gaps.what_would_fill_it: A national volunteering passport: shared vetting and digital credential standard letting volunteers move between organisations without repeat checks; funded employer-supported volunteering incentives; and a voluntary civic service year for 16–24s with accreditation and bursaries, delivered through existing youth organisations rather than a new quango.

gaps.title: No transition mechanism turning closing faith buildings into community assets
gaps.description: Faith buildings are the densest network of civic space in many neighbourhoods, and they are failing: 969 places of worship sit on Historic England's Heritage at Risk Register; the Listed Places of Worship VAT scheme closed on 31 March 2026 (repairs now attract 20% VAT), replaced by a smaller £92m/4-year, England-only Places of Worship Renewal Fund. As congregations shrink and buildings close, there is no standard route into community ownership: the Churches Conservation Trust and Friends of Friendless Churches preserve rather than repurpose; the Community Right to Buy applies only to listed assets actually offered for sale; National Churches Trust campaigns but cannot broker transfers. Each conversion is a bespoke, multi-year legal struggle with denominational property rules.
gaps.why_it_matters: Thousands of centrally-located, often heritage-grade civic buildings will change hands over the next two decades. Without a transfer mechanism, they will be lost to housing conversion or dereliction precisely as neighbourhood programmes search for venues — a one-time, irreversible loss of social infrastructure in low-income and rural areas.
gaps.what_would_fill_it: A Faith Buildings Transition Agency and endowed fund (heritage bodies + denominations + dormant assets): standardised legal templates for transfer from denominational trusts to community ownership, feasibility grants, and blended capital for conversion to mixed worship/community use — analogous to how the Plunkett model professionalised community pub and shop rescues.

gaps.title: Place-based philanthropy outside London lacks endowed capital
gaps.description: London receives more than a third of large-foundation funding and four times the Gift Aid value of the UK average; total giving (~£14bn, 2025) is heavily skewed by geography. The government's first place-based philanthropy strategy, Our Place to Give (April 2026), is directionally right but backed by just £1m over three years for a community of practice. The 47 community foundations are the natural vehicle but most hold modest endowments; the last national endowment-match programme (Community First) ended in 2015. The Better Futures Fund (£500m) buys outcomes rather than building permanent local capital; Big Local's £1m-per-area endowment-like model ends in 2026.
gaps.why_it_matters: Civic renewal in left-behind places needs patient, locally-controlled money that survives political cycles — exactly what endowment provides and grant programmes do not. The current strategy asks philanthropy to rebalance geographically without any fiscal instrument to make it happen, so the London skew will persist.
gaps.what_would_fill_it: A ten-year national endowment match challenge (£200–300m Treasury/dormant-assets match for donations to community foundation endowments in low-giving areas, echoing the successful 2011–15 Community First match), plus Gift Aid reform options and legacy-giving incentives tied to place; delivered through UK Community Foundations with published local giving data.

gaps.title: Community organising has no long-term core-funding vehicle
gaps.description: The UK organising field remains subscale and precariously financed. Citizens UK (19 chapters, 100+ staff) relies on member dues plus project grants and one large Lottery Solidarity Fund award (~£5m over nine years); ACORN's dues-funded model sustains branches in only ~10 cities; Community Organisers Ltd trains but cannot fund organisers' salaries. The state's Community Organisers Programme (500 trained, 2011–15) and its expansion ended without succession. Foundations fund organising through short project grants that conflict with the model's need for patient, independent core money — organising wins (living wage, housing campaigns) take 5–10 years. No UK equivalent exists of the US's dedicated organising philanthropy intermediaries.
gaps.why_it_matters: Organising is the proven method for rebuilding associational power among people institutions have stopped reaching — the exact populations where volunteering, membership and trust have fallen furthest. Fifty organisers cost less than one regeneration scheme, yet the field cannot plan beyond eighteen months anywhere outside its strongest chapters.
gaps.what_would_fill_it: A pooled ten-year Community Organising Fund (foundations + dormant assets, deliberately not government-controlled to protect independence) paying core salaries for organisers in 50+ places, with a common training standard via the National Academy of Community Organising and published measures of membership and leadership development.

funders: National Lottery Community Fund (largest community funder; Community Wealth Fund delivery; Solidarity Fund)
Dormant Assets Scheme (community wealth, youth, financial inclusion strands — most plausible source of new civic capital)
DCMS (Covenant programme, Know Your Neighbourhood, Amplify local media plan, Better Futures Fund, place-based philanthropy roadmap)
MHCLG (Pride in Place £5bn, neighbourhood governance implementation)
BBC licence fee (Local Democracy Reporting Service, subject to Charter review)
Esmée Fairbairn Foundation
Joseph Rowntree Foundation / Joseph Rowntree Reform Trust
Lloyds Bank Foundation for England & Wales
Paul Hamlyn Foundation
City Bridge Foundation (London)
Power to Change (community business)
People's Health Trust (community power, We're Right Here)
UK community foundations network (47 foundations, place-based giving)
Nesta / think-tank-adjacent innovation funders
International democracy funders active in UK civic tech (National Endowment for Democracy, Luminate)
Corporate/platform money (Google News Initiative — contested; potential platform levy)
domains.policy_notes: The Covenant (July 2025) is non-statutory with no adjudication mechanism; its Local Covenant Partnerships Fund (£11.59m) reaches only 15 English council areas. The English Devolution and Community Empowerment Act 2026 delivered one of the three community rights campaigners sought (buy — not shape-services or control-investment) and leaves neighbourhood-governance parameters to future regulations. Government closed the Community Ownership Fund without a capital successor, betting the right alone suffices. The Lobbying Act 2014 remains unreformed despite documented chilling effects; CIVICUS has rated UK civic space 'obstructed' since 2023 and HRW (January 2026) documents escalating protest restrictions — an awkward counterpoint to partnership rhetoric. Loneliness policy has drifted since 2020 (DCMS team repurposed; evidence bodies closed). Local news policy is modest (Amplify: £12m over two years) with LDRS funding unresolved in Charter review. Dormant assets and the new Office for the Impact Economy are the live levers to watch.

domains.domain: Open source tools and public goods
domains.landscape: The UK was an early leader: the 2012 Service Standard and Technology Code of Practice made government code "open by default", GDS's alphagov GitHub became a global reference, and OpenUK finds open source contributes 27% of UK digital GVA, with the UK the top OSS contributor in Europe. By mid-2026 policy and practice have diverged sharply. NHS England quietly deleted its open source policy pages (December 2025) and in May 2026 adopted a default-closed posture on its code over AI security concerns, prompting an open letter from 70+ signatories. data.gov.uk's own team admits over 25% of links are broken after "underinvestment since 2017"; GDS's canonical Registers programme was retired in 2021. There is no UK analogue of Germany's Sovereign Tech Fund (over €24.6m deployed to 60+ critical open source projects), and the EU dropped Next Generation Internet from Horizon Europe's 2025 work programme, closing the NLnet small-grant route. Counter-currents exist: the Software Sustainability Institute's £4.8m UKRI Research Software Maintenance Fund (2025) is the first of its kind; LocalGov Drupal's steward, Open Digital Cooperative, is profitably self-sustaining across ~60 councils; OpenSAFELY won £17m over seven years from Wellcome; DSIT's National Data Library completed discovery with £100m+ backing; UKRI contracted OpenUK (October 2025) to draft public-sector open source guidance; and GDS Local is consulting on an open source assurance process. Government money, however, flows to sovereign AI (a £500m equity fund) rather than the open infrastructure underneath it.

actors.name: OpenUK
actors.note: Advocacy and research body for UK open technology; publishes State of Open reports (27% of digital GVA claim); contracted by UKRI in October 2025 to write public-sector open source guidance including a sovereign tech fund recommendation.

actors.name: Government Digital Service (GDS, within DSIT)
actors.note: Owns the Service Standard and Technology Code of Practice 'open by default' rules; GDS Local's 'Sourcing the Stack' (May 2026) is building the first cross-government open source assurance criteria.

actors.name: Department for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT)
actors.note: Owns the National Data Library, data.gov.uk reset, Software Security Code of Practice and commissioned OSS supply-chain research; currently funds sovereign AI, not open infrastructure.

actors.name: Software Sustainability Institute (SSI)
actors.note: Runs the £4.8m UKRI Research Software Maintenance Fund (13 projects in round 1, 2025; round 2 opened January 2026) — the UK's only dedicated software maintenance fund.

actors.name: Open Digital Cooperative (LocalGov Drupal)
actors.note: Steward of LocalGov Drupal, used by ~60 councils and 23 vendors; became self-sustaining on member subscriptions after ~£1m of DLUHC Local Digital Fund grants ended in 2023; councils save £30k-£90k per website rebuild.

actors.name: Bennett Institute for Applied Data Science (University of Oxford)
actors.note: Builds OpenSAFELY and OpenPrescribing, open source NHS data infrastructure; long-running funding precarity eased by £17m from Wellcome (2025), illustrating dependence on philanthropy rather than NHS core budgets.

actors.name: Open Data Institute (ODI)
actors.note: Founded 2012 by Berners-Lee and Shadbolt; data stewardship training, standards and policy work; fed evidence into the National Data Library discovery phase.

actors.name: mySociety / SocietyWorks
actors.note: Runs TheyWorkForYou, WhatDoTheyKnow and FixMyStreet — de facto national civic infrastructure funded by foundations plus commercial income, with no dedicated UK funding stream for such infrastructure.

actors.name: Centre for Public Data
actors.note: Anna Powell-Smith's non-partisan data-policy body; leads the Open Address File UK campaign and has asked Ofcom to review Royal Mail's Postcode Address File licensing.

actors.name: Society of Research Software Engineering
actors.note: Professional association for the UK's research software engineers; the RSE career track the UK invented still lacks recurring maintenance funding.

actors.name: National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC)
actors.note: Publishes SBOM and supply-chain guidance and warns of active dependency-compromise attacks, but runs no inventory of or support scheme for the open source the state depends on.

actors.name: Apperta Foundation
actors.note: Clinician-led CIC promoting open platforms in health (steward of the OpenEyes EPR); still operating but small, and now swimming against NHS England's 2026 default-closed code posture.

actors.name: UKRI / Innovate UK
actors.note: Funds the Digital Research Infrastructure programme (host of the SSI maintenance fund) and is the administering body proposed for a UK open-source fund by both OpenUK and the UK Day One paper.

actors.name: National Data Library (DSIT programme)
actors.note: £100m+ programme; completed discovery January 2026 with five kickstarter projects; absorbing data.gov.uk and shifting it from passive aggregation to curation; details due spring 2026.

actors.name: Open Rights Group
actors.note: Running a 'Demand UK Digital Sovereignty' campaign pushing government away from proprietary hyperscaler dependence toward open alternatives.

gaps.title: No UK Sovereign Tech Fund for maintaining critical open source infrastructure
gaps.description: Germany's Sovereign Tech Fund (Sovereign Tech Agency) has invested over €24.6m in 60+ critical open source components since 2022, and a €350m EU-level fund is proposed; the UK, whose software stacks are 90%+ OSS-dependent (Log4j hit the NHS), has no equivalent. Partial coverage: SSI's £4.8m Research Software Maintenance Fund covers research software only; OpenSSF's Alpha-Omega is US corporate philanthropy not UK-directed; corporate sponsorship is ad hoc. A costed blueprint exists — the UK Day One proposal (Milton, Osborne, Pickering, 2024) for a £12.5m/year UKRI-administered fund (£7.5m maintenance, £5m innovation) — and OpenUK's October 2025 recommendations to UKRI include a sovereign tech fund. Nothing has been announced as of mid-2026; the £500m Sovereign AI Fund is an equity venture fund, not commons maintenance.
gaps.why_it_matters: The UK state and economy free-ride on unpaid maintainers of components they cannot function without. One funded UK maintainer per critical dependency is cheap insurance against the next Log4j; Germany has proven the delivery model works at €15-20m/year.
gaps.what_would_fill_it: A 'UK Sovereign Tech Fund' inside DSIT or UKRI at £10-15m/year, issuing service contracts (not grants) to maintainers of dependencies critical to government and CNI, with a rapid security-response arm; adopt the Sovereign Tech Agency's published operating model and the UK Day One costing.

gaps.title: No cross-government Open Source Programme Office to own the 'open by default' policy
gaps.description: France, Germany (ZenDiS) and the European Commission run public-sector OSPOs; the UK — which wrote the open-by-default playbook — has none. Ownership is fragmented between GDS (Service Standard point on open code), the Technology Code of Practice and individual departments; nobody audits whether departments actually publish code, curates cross-government reuse, or owns licensing and stewardship questions. The cost of this vacuum showed when NHS England's open source policies silently lapsed after the NHSX merger and the pages were deleted in December 2025. OpenUK's UKRI-commissioned guidance (2025-26) is advisory and GDS Local's work is nascent; neither is an accountable institution.
gaps.why_it_matters: A policy without an owner evaporates, as the NHS just demonstrated. An OSPO converts fourteen years of aspiration into measured compliance, reuse savings, and a single door for maintainers, vendors and international peers to knock on.
gaps.what_would_fill_it: A cross-government OSPO in GDS/DSIT with a mandate to audit Service Standard compliance annually, publish a code-reuse catalogue, steward government-maintained projects, and represent the UK in international public-code networks; modelled on the EC OSPO and ZenDiS.

gaps.title: No national assurance service giving public bodies confidence in open source products
gaps.description: GDS Principal Technologist Phil Rumens stated in May 2026 that there is 'no formal or standardised process across government for evaluating, recommending, or providing confidence in open-source products'. Every council, trust and department repeats (or skips) its own due diligence, so risk-averse buyers default to proprietary suppliers who arrive pre-assured. GDS Local's 'Sourcing the Stack' is consulting on seven evaluation criteria (maturity, governance, security, sustainability, cost, operational fit, ethical AI) but is early-stage and unfunded as a service; Open Digital Cooperative assures only LocalGov Drupal.
gaps.why_it_matters: LocalGov Drupal shows the prize — 30-50% cost reductions and £30k-£90k saved per council website — but only one product ever crossed the assurance barrier. A kite-mark service would unlock those savings across the whole local government and NHS software stack.
gaps.what_would_fill_it: Fund GDS Local's assurance process into a standing national service: kite-marked evaluations, pooled security reviews, a public catalogue of assured open source products with signposting to commercial support vendors — the equivalent of Germany's openCode platform.

gaps.title: No open national address file: PAF remains a proprietary Royal Mail asset
gaps.description: The Postcode Address File (32.1m delivery addresses) went private with Royal Mail in 2013; in 2025 Royal Mail successfully defended its database rights in court, and anyone needing address data pays layered licence fees under complex IP restrictions. Partial substitutes are inadequate: ONS Postcode Directory gives postcode centroids not addresses; Ordnance Survey opened UPRNs (identifiers) in 2020 but not the addresses themselves; OpenStreetMap coverage is incomplete. The ODI-incubated Open Addresses project died in 2015 for lack of legal access. The Centre for Public Data's Open Address File UK campaign and a House of Lords proposal have put the issue back on the agenda, including a request that Ofcom review PAF licensing.
gaps.why_it_matters: Address lookup is a tax on every delivery firm, emergency service, council and startup in the country. Nations with open address files (Denmark, France, Netherlands) documented net economic gains; the UK re-buys its own geography millions of times over.
gaps.what_would_fill_it: Government acquisition or mandated open licensing of PAF (Ofcom review of PAF terms as the first step), or an openly licensed national address register built from councils' AddressBase source data and published through the National Data Library.

gaps.title: No statutory duty or function for maintaining canonical government reference data
gaps.description: GDS's Registers programme — 51 canonical, API-accessible datasets (countries, local authorities, schools) — was retired in March 2021 with no successor function. The consequences are now official: data.gov.uk's own team admits over 25% of links lead to error pages, datasets sit unupdated for years, and publishers include organisations that no longer exist, after 'underinvestment since 2017'. The March 2026 data.gov.uk reset (curation, Data Manual v1, public roadmap) treats symptoms, but no department is under any duty to maintain reference data that others build on, and the National Data Library's published plans do not yet include ownership obligations.
gaps.why_it_matters: Every service, journalist and AI system built on rotting reference data inherits its errors. The government is now spending £100m+ on a National Data Library whose raw inputs decay by default because maintenance is nobody's job.
gaps.what_would_fill_it: A registers function inside the National Data Library: named accountable data owners per canonical dataset, update SLAs and public quality dashboards, backed by a Cabinet Office functional standard or statutory duty to maintain designated reference datasets.

gaps.title: No UK small-grants vehicle for public-interest internet technology (NGI analogue)
gaps.description: The EU's Next Generation Internet initiative distributed ~€140m to 1,200+ projects in small, low-bureaucracy cascade grants (largely via NLnet) for privacy tech, open protocols and digital commons — then the Commission dropped NGI from the Horizon Europe 2025 work programme. UK developers had already lost easy access post-Brexit, and no domestic substitute exists at the €5k-€50k grant size where most independent maintainers and public-interest tools live. UK civil-society funders explicitly treat technology as unfundable infrastructure (Careful Industries, December 2025), and Nesta/National Lottery digital funds have closed. Careful Industries has floated a 'Civic Technology Fund' concept; nobody has built it.
gaps.why_it_matters: Small fast grants are the proven mechanism that produced much of Europe's privacy and protocol innovation. With both the EU spigot and UK philanthropy closed, an entire tier of public-interest tooling now has no funder in the UK at all.
gaps.what_would_fill_it: A pooled DSIT-philanthropy fund making £10k-£75k grants with NLnet-style lightweight process, targeting privacy-enhancing tech, open protocols, accessibility and digital commons; could be hosted by an existing intermediary (e.g. ODI or Nesta) to avoid new-institution overhead.

gaps.title: Research software maintenance funding is a one-off pilot, not a recurring budget line
gaps.description: The SSI's Research Software Maintenance Fund — £4.8m from UKRI's Digital Research Infrastructure programme, first round 2025 — is the UK's first dedicated maintenance fund and proves demand: 13 projects funded (four up to £500k/two years, nine up to £150k/one year), with round 2 capped at £150k for 12 months. But it is a fixed-term pilot against a research software estate of thousands of packages underpinning UK science; when the DRI spending period ends there is no recurring UKRI line for keeping software alive, and EPSRC RSE fellowships have been sporadic. Grant incentives still reward novelty over stewardship, pushing the RSE workforce (organised by the Society of Research Software Engineering) onto short contracts.
